#141 Life lessons at Mount Jimibong, Korea

Areas discussed: Jeju South Korea, Seogwipo, Jeju Olle Trail, Jimibong, Hado Beach, Byeolbangjin Fortress

June 25, 2025 1 hour 38 minutes Jeju Island, South Korea

All transcripts are machine generated.

I'm like, all right, maybe it's a little much to go around the loop that way. But what if I started going around the loop the other way? And it's like a little awkward because the the 201 bus doesn't become the 202 bus here in Seiguipo. It is a bit west of here. So that's going to make that part of the journey a little weird in general that I'm going to have to transfer every time. But I was like, yeah, I could do that. Now that I know how the buses work, I could hop on the bus, I could go out there, I could start going the other way. But it's like, yeah, just fucking relax. You fucking weirdo. Just take a fucking day off. You were fucking sick shit last night. Come on. You were trying to make yourself puke. That's how bad it was. Take a day off as whole. So I'm going to take a day off. Just wander around this Isol neighborhood. Yeah, I really do like it here. It really has been nice in South Korea. How, you know, I made my begrudging piece with soul enough so that I do want to go back sometime. And it's really, it's just 35:00 Bukhonson. It's Bukhonson alone. If it wasn't for Bukhonson National Park, I'd be done with soul. It'd be like, no, you're good soul. You do you. But that park is so amazing. And those mountains are so cool that I could see going back just for that. So I'm glad I at least hit that level.

And Jetshon, similar, you know, I probably will go back to Jetshon sometime just for the fuck of it. And I will start exploring a little more with the buses. There is some stuff around there. It's one of those things, you know, maybe I'll only go back one time.

But then getting to Bukhonson right away, I was like, fuck yeah, all right, now I'm feeling good. And then Juju is just, it's kind of not exciting like Bukhonson is, you know, if I was to live somewhere for for real and true, maybe it would be Bukhonson. But to just live somewhere for a summer, man, Juju is good. It's just so comfy, you know. It's like, it's beautiful, tropical, amazing island, but also kind of not exciting, you know, just that weird lack of that lack of action, that lack of energy is kind of what's nice. It's just a chill place. It's just cool. So yeah, taking the day off, just hanging out in my little neighborhood, fuck it. Fuck it, not wrong with that. All it needs, man. All it needs is for that one edia coffee that was supposed to be in Segwapo to still be open. And it would just be perfect.

Anyway, so I'm just heading forward and we'll see if I connect back up to the shore. It's kind of cool too now that I've got this better sense of what heights mean. Because when I first got to Japan, I had no idea. But just that I've been paying attention. And now I'm starting to understand. And it seems like everything around here is about 200 meters or a little less.

And that made me out of curiosity. Look up Mount Royal last night. And Mount Royal in Montreal is about 230 or something somewhere around there. And then I also learned about prominence. Kind of the more important number is the prominence, which is like how high is a given mountain based on what's around it. And the prominence of Mount Royal is like 227. It's like almost the same. It's like super prominent. Everything in Montreal is roughly sea level. And then there's this giant fucking mountain. Boom. Because yeah, a lot of these numbers are a little weird. They're a little the pure height of the mountain. It really is not as relevant as the prominence. But it's kind of hard to find the prominence number sometimes. Yeah, this is a pretty fucking nice. My man pretty fucking nice.

Oh, I found a convenience store. How fucking delightful. I got to. Usually when they have like the buy two get one freeze, it's pretty well labeled in Korean convenience stores. And they're a big fan of that. A lot of buy one get one free buy two get one free. But it moves around a lot. You know, it might not be a snickers bar today. It might be something else or whatever. But yeah, one thing I thought was kind of interesting when I got off that last little mini mountain with the cool view. How I was saying that the trail, the Ola trail sent me up the mountain rather than through all of the bridges. But then when I got to the bottom, then it led me to the bridges. But that's going exactly the wrong way. So I just said I know thank you. Because that's the other nice thing being by the coast. It's so easy to just not follow the arrows. I mean the coast is right there. Just follow the coast instead. So I just went the other way along the coast and quickly rejoined up to the arrows. So what happened there? I guess just because this must be why trail number one. They said took four to five hours when I walked that stretch in like half an hour because it really does send you off in a bunch of places. So I got to assume that's the one it would have sent me up further into the mainland. Then down around to this geo walkway place and to the view I was at. So you could see Ciansan before you go. Then it takes you down along the sand bar and then around on that other bridge to escape that it turns out does have a walking bit. 55:00 So I did this whole bit out of order. But again, no big deal. You know, the coast is always there. So who cares. But this is also starting to explain why I thought it was so weird that the circumnavigate, the circumference of the island is only 180 kilometers if you just follow the road and only 200 some if you just follow the actual coastline. How is the Ola trail over 400? What the fuck. And this is starting to explain that. It seems that the part I was at going counterclockwise starting at Seguapo. There just didn't happen to be a lot of variation for that whole walk for the whole first like five or six days I was walking. The Ola trail and the coastline were basically one to one. That's really just all it was just follow the coast mainly. So this Ciansan part is the first part where it is massively deviated and so much so that yet sends you way up into the inland then brings you around. It's not only to see all the stuff, but to see it in the proper order. You introduce you to what's coming up next and then looping back around on itself and then out around on to Ciansan. You know, it's like a whole whole thing that if you did just follow it directly, it'd be fine. It'd probably be pretty cool. But yeah, I just didn't and it's just nice that you don't have to. It's so easy like this is such a nice walk because it's got all the cool arrows. But if you want to bail out on the arrows, it's just no problem at all. Just follow the real coastline. And the arrows will be there. They'll be there before long. You'll just reconnect.

Pretty cool. And yeah, this mountain, instead of just calling it the mountain that's coming up, can I figure out what this fucking thing is called? Because it's very prominent now. You know, it's pretty big. It's in the distance. It's only 330.

I feel like I should be able to make it there and make it up today.

But I'm sure it's farther than it seems. You know, at the very least, I will make it there. And then I could start there next time. But I feel like I'll probably make it up there. And yeah, it's been pretty nice with clouds keep stopping by, covering up the sun right now. There's clouds over top of me. They kind of come and go. But even just an occasional respite from the sun goes a long fucking way.

Makes the day a lot better. So yeah, this is nice. So back on the trail, mountain ahead of me, nothing else visible behind it, just more coastline.

So just walk and walk and walk. You know, I had a, I think because I took three whole days off, which is a very long break on this little journey. The first was just a normal day off. Then I took a second day off because it was call home day. So I needed to be home very early in the evening.

And then the third day was food poisoning day. So I think my brain was into this. It was like, we got to get back on this. We got to get back on the journey. It's so cool. It's just so fun to walk around the outside of the island. What are you doing? Why's it been so long? So I had a dream. But man, the dream was weird. It was like, I mean, what's the point of describing dreams? But it was the basic setting of I'm walking to the next leg of the journey. And I knew there was a mountain coming up. But then it became like, that was finding, maybe this was because the hotel was replacing all of its stuff. Maybe that's why this was in my mind. But it was like furniture and stuff. I was finding all this furniture that seemed weirdly new. Yeah, it seems like a pretty direct relation now that I say it out loud. But that turned into some kind of detective mystery of like some people went missing and the clues are in the furniture. But somehow I was discovering the furniture and trying to solve the mystery while walking along the outside of the island. Conceptually, it makes no fucking sense.

But I guess just the fact that I started having dreams about this walk is notable. That's a pretty good sign that I probably should just end out my time in South Korea on Jeju. Just to make sure I can make it the whole way, because this is clearly what my brain wants to do. It's just fun. It's just nice.

This view of the mountain ahead of me, it's so clear. Like I'm just walking along basically. I would say a sand bar, but there's no sand. A rock bar with just a road going through it. And a bunch of algae and kelp and stuff, or kelp looking stuff in the water. And all I can see is just some hotels and then some houses and then that mountain. No washrooms is what I'm trying to say. No evidence of a washroom coming up anytime soon. And in fact, I haven't found one this whole fucking time. I secretly snuck away into the woods when I was at that cool lookout and just took a quick pee. Because I don't know what else to fucking do. So what I'm trying to say is I'm taking my bladder into my hands here, which is a gross thing to say. By drinking this Starbucks coffee, because it's medium big, 275 milliliters. And I don't fancy my chances of being able to pee anytime soon. But whatever, we're going to do it. And we're going to do it. Cross your fingers for me. Cross your cocks.

Well, it just passed a young foreign lad. Probably in his, he looked like he was in like his early 20s and gave each other a nice hello, but man, I'm always impressed by that because I did some traveling within Canada at that age, you know, I moved to Vancouver. That was felt pretty epic to me. Then by my late 20s, I moved to New York, but I barely helped. I was held on by my fucking fingertips. I did not integrate well with that. And then I finally went overseas and like my mid 30s and freaked the fuck out. Total culture shock, bailed out came home. All this going to Asia and crazy shit. It doesn't seem realistic that I would have been able to pull any of this off until I was in my late 30s and now my early 40s, you know.

I don't feel too bad. I don't beat myself up about the fact that I didn't travel more when I was young because I just don't think I really could have done it. But when I see other people who do, it's fucking impressive. Nice work. But this guy, I mean, he was going the other way, so he came down off this mountain just now. He's following the clockwise route. And he's all kidded out. He's got the double hiking poles, which I feel like everywhere else in Korea, I'd say for sure.

Actually, those, I mean, I kind of enjoyed doing just the four-limbed scramble. But it would have made sense to have poles in a lot of places. I was thinking about how in Buchanan, when there was snow up there, I was literally sliding down the snow hundreds of meters up a few times. Not the smartest thing probably. But on this particular walk, perhaps the hiking poles are a little much, but what this guy did have, they made a lot of sense in his full hiking kit. As he was wearing a hat that was almost like a beekeeper's hat. Almost, it was what it reminded me of. I don't know, it was just like a big wide brimmed hat that had probably as like a bug net, but that was rolled up.

Anyway, that makes a lot of sense. I just need a great big hat. Because yeah, I do have to put, I forgot to do it today, actually. Like I do have to put suntan lotion on my ears because they're poking out of my little hat. A big hat would cover up my neck. However, am I going to buy a big hat? Probably not.

Hats are surprisingly expensive. If I could get a cheap dollar store hat just to wear while I'm on Jeju and then abandon it. But I mean, it's all relative again. A lot of people would not consider 20 bucks a lot, but I feel like that's a lot just to wear a hat for a month and then leave it here.

Because I know I am carrying a big ass hat around with me. But yeah, that's interesting. That's just see the fucking answer literally walk towards you later in the day. It's like, oh, oh, that's what you're supposed to do. That's what proper hiker people with all the right equipment. That's what they do.

And the other thing that I saw that was kind of cool is on that rocky sand bar part.

There was a bunch of stuff hung up on strings as I walked along that kind of looked like is if someone was hanging up their socks to dry on a clothesline or something. But as I got closer, I realized they were squid. People just catch squid in that bay, I guess, and hang them up right there. There's like a hundred of them. Just squid hanging there to dry like socks, like edible socks. Yeah, just kind of neat little things I've never seen that before. I may never see it again.

Asia is eternally the gift that keeps on giving. I wonder like it's possible I suppose that other countries could be this way too. I feel like just based on the example of the Netherlands. All of the European countries in that area, whenever I see clips of them and stuff, they sure do look remarkably similar to one another. But maybe, I don't know who knows, maybe if I went to these different places, they would stand out. But I always think of Russia. If only Russia had not become fully insane. I mean, actually, I probably never would have gone to Russia anyways, just ahead of bad vibe. The place just seemed creepy. But especially not now, you know, fuck them. I don't even know if you can go visit Russia, but I wouldn't. I feel like they're on that list of North Korea. Just don't support these fucking places.

Which, I mean, hey, again, not like it's tough to do, because they don't want you to come in anyway.

You couldn't go if you wanted to, but I have a feeling you'd have a lot of these stories in Russia. These times when, you know, just like where it's like a glitch in the matrix, like the VR suit is not working right. Like, just things are just weird. The stories I've heard about Russia are so fucking weird. The wild dogs in the subways, and the kids getting high on glue. And just, I don't know how this fucked up shit.

Korea's nice because I still pretty often have these moments where it's just something I never could have planned for. Nothing I could have guessed was going to happen. I never thought I'd see a bunch of squid just pinned up to dry in the wind, but it just happens. You're like, huh, there's a weird thing. There's a thing I didn't expect to see.

But I guess what's nice about Korea as opposed to like when you hear stories about Russia or North Korea, where it's like crazy shit that makes no sense. In Korea, it's like crazy shit that does make sense. It's like, oh yeah, okay. I mean, it makes sense that that would happen. Why not? Even stuff like the wild dogs living in the fucking mountains. It makes sense. You just just unexpected. That's all, you know.

All right, arrows. Where are you? Oh, there you are. I'm getting real close to this mountain now. I'm almost there.

And it's only four o'clock, so yeah, I'll make it up there for sure.

The clouds have passed. It's hot as fuck again. But there's no guarantee it's going to be any better tomorrow. It might be worse, because I would probably get here earlier in the day.

So I think we're doing this today. Oh, speaking of though, now that my blue dot is closer and closer. See if I can figure out what this goddamn place is called.

Jimmy Bong, J-I-M-I-P-O-N-G, man. I mean, I know I've said this a few times, but obviously the names of stuff, Korean stuff, has way different meanings to them. But man, all these names are very bizarre sounding when switched over to English, but that's got to be the weirdest one yet. Jimmy Bong. Jimmy Bong. Jimmy Bong. Rrr-r-r-ra, mama say, mama say, mama, makusa.

But yeah, I'm heading to Jimmy Bong.

Oh, there's quite a big mountain in the distance I can see from here, but I can't tell if it's at all close to the coast, so that might not be something that I go to at all, and it looks really big. It looks unpleasantly big, but there's something out there. So I guess even without the 2,000 meter crazy fucking volcano mountain, there's still lots of mountains to hike around here. So in this case, unlike with Cian Sun, where the arrows were going well out of their way to guide you there, not the case with Jimmy Bong. They just took me straight by, so as soon as I realized that I was not being led here, I looped back around and just headed straight to the mountain, and it's just like, well, it's just over the best. Let's just try to figure this out. So I saw a little trail and headed toward it, but a halfway here, I realized, oh, this is not right, because I was just 1:10:00 basically walking through like what must be farmer fields, and there seems to be nothing going on with them right now. They don't seem like they have anything planted, but I still, I walked along the edges, not to tromp across anything, but as like, oh, shit, I'm not supposed to be here for sure. But I quickly made my way through, and now I'm on a path, and this looks a lot more official now. There's even a light here. So this is not, I didn't find the proper entrance. I don't know, and wait a second. So yeah, now I'm walking up this thing and yeah, there's yet another light. So wow, could you actually go up here at night? Crazy, I didn't expect that. But what the fuck are these now? Is that a little ribbon, a little orange ribbon? So maybe, I guess this might be one of those things. Yeah, that's totally an Olay Trail ribbon. There's no blue one, just an orange one. But I did see that there's places on the Olay Trail where there's optional parts. That must be what this is. This must be one of the optional parts. I was following the main trail, and it was just taking me away. But I think maybe it's just because, I guess, just following purely from the arrows and stuff. It's just not quite enough sometimes.
